Psychographic Research for Effective Sales
Copy
This document explores the RMBC method for copywriting, where the R stands for research. We'll examine how to conduct
deep psychographic research to understand your target market's struggles, pain points, and beliefs. By leveraging multiple AI
tools like Gemini, Perplexity, and ChatGPT, we can gather comprehensive insights that inform compelling sales copy. The
document includes real-world examples for skincare products targeting women 40+, Airbnb arbitrage opportunities, and golf
products, demonstrating how to apply this research framework across different markets.

-- 1 of 8 --

Understanding Your Customer: The Inner
World
Your customer is a U.S. woman aged 40+ experiencing visible signs of aging including wrinkles, dark spots, and puffy eye bags.
She represents a unique and demanding life stage - in her peak earning years with higher disposable income than younger
generations, yet caught between societal expectations of youthful appearance and the natural aging process.
A defining characteristic is her role as a member of the "sandwich generation" - simultaneously raising her own children while
caring for aging parents. This dual responsibility creates immense pressure, making work-life balance a central priority. She
functions as the de facto "chief medical officer" of her household, developing sophisticated medical and nutritional literacy
while managing healthcare for her family.
She is not a passive consumer but a research-driven, analytical individual who values quality, authenticity, and transparency.
Technologically proficient, she primarily uses established platforms like Facebook and Instagram for connection and
information gathering, though she may feel overwhelmed by constant digital innovations.
In Her Own Words
"I guess this is not specifically a
menopause question, just a
general getting old and ugly and
seeking advice on skin care to
reduce lines on my face,
especially upper lips."
Identity Crisis
"I was in my mid-50s when I
started going through
menopause - the fluctuating
hormones caused outbreaks of
acne on my face, especially
around my chin area. I thought I
left this way back in my teenage
years, so it was a confidence
knock to worry about bad skin
again."
Betrayal of Time
"Nobody told me how quickly
things can go downhill. I was
actually attractive back then.
'Looks aren't important' was
easy to say when I was young."

-- 2 of 8 --

Attitudes, Hopes, and Dreams
This demographic has evolved a complex set of attitudes shaped by their life experiences. They are highly research-driven
consumers, less swayed by flashy marketing and more influenced by peer recommendations and demonstrable results. Their
focus on health and wellness has matured beyond just looking good to feeling good and ensuring long-term vitality.
Economically, they are stable but cautious, valuing quality and durability while showing preference for brands that align with
their ethical priorities.
Many turn to spiritual practices in middle age, seeking deeper meaning. They tend to challenge traditional aging stereotypes
while simultaneously feeling pressure from beauty standards. Research shows that 99% of women over 50 are afraid of aging,
yet 22% feel less pressure to meet beauty standards compared to younger women.
Hopes and Dreams
Feeling confident, powerful, and comfortable in their
own skin
Looking as young as they feel, or at least feeling
refreshed and confident
Pursuing new adventures and personal growth
opportunities
Maintaining professional relevance and advancement
Achieving work-life balance while caring for family
In Her Own Words
"I focused on enhancing skin strength, boosting collagen
and elastin production. I'm more useful from an
appearance standpoint, especially looking less
weathered or rough."
"My skin looks more alive, my face often lifted, and my
eyes don't feel so droopy anymore."
"76% say they're actually trying to embrace aging with
grace and confidence."

-- 3 of 8 --

Victories and Failures: The Psychological
Tension
The greatest victory for women in this demographic is internal - a surge in confidence that is well-documented by research.
Studies show that while women are often less confident than men in their 20s and 30s, they not only catch up by age 40 but
continue to grow in confidence, eventually surpassing their male counterparts by age 60. This is a source of immense pride,
along with career advancement, financial stability, and resilience developed through life experience.
However, their perceived failures are deeply personal and often feel like a betrayal by their own body. The most profound
struggle arises when the health, diet, and exercise regimens that worked for decades suddenly stop working. The hormonal
shifts of perimenopause and menopause bring about changes - wrinkles, fatigue, skin issues - that feel entirely out of their
control.
The Core Psychological
Tension
Internally, she's more confident,
wise, disciplined, and powerful than
ever before. Externally, she feels
her appearance is declining in ways
that defy her control and discipline.
This is not simple vanity - it is a
profound and deeply felt injustice
that her physical self no longer
reflects the vibrant, capable, and
resilient woman she has become.
In Her Own Words
"The effortless attention my looks
garnered got me addicted to being
pretty. I've tried things that are
ineffective. Nobody told me how
fast things can go downhill."
"This shit is hard. I really thought I
was going to skate through the
whole thing gracefully."
The Emotional Impact
"How are other ladies coping with
losing your looks and maybe no
longer turning heads in the way you
used to? I wouldn't say I feel
invisible yet, but going out
definitely takes longer and more
effort."
"You start to think that you're
really falling apart."

-- 4 of 8 --

External Forces and Prejudices
While she is frustrated by societal beauty standards that glorify youth, her most acute blame is directed at two primary forces.
First is her own biology, specifically the tumultuous and often unspoken challenges of perimenopause and menopause, which
she feels unprepared for and abandoned in. Second is a medical community that she believes has systematically dismissed,
under-researched, and misunderstood women's health.
She feels profoundly slighted that no one adequately warned her about the physical and mental challenges of this life stage.
This feeling of being unheard and dismissed has cultivated a deep-seated prejudice against being patronized or having her
experience minimized.
Hormonal Changes
The biological shifts of perimenopause and menopause
that cause dramatic skin changes despite maintaining the
same skincare routines.
Medical Dismissal
The healthcare system's historical neglect of women's
health issues, particularly those related to aging and
menopause.
Societal Ageism
Cultural obsession with youth that makes women feel
increasingly invisible as they age, despite their growing
confidence and capabilities.
Social Media Pressure
Unrealistic beauty standards perpetuated by filtered
images and youth-focused content that exacerbate
feelings of inadequacy.
Prejudices and Skepticism
Her life experiences have armed her with a healthy set of prejudices. She is deeply skeptical of gimmicky marketing, shallow
social media influencers, and brands that lack transparency and authenticity. She harbors a strong suspicion of "miracle
creams" and often holds the belief that simple, old-fashioned solutions are more effective than expensive, overhyped products.
"I'm not the type of woman to blow big money on useless products and then delude myself that they are making a
difference."
"Some of my friends are beginning to do things to their face, and I often think the botox and fillers make their face look
unnatural and definitely do not look better than when they were younger."

-- 5 of 8 --

Market Experience: What Works and What
Doesn't
The 40+ woman's approach to skincare is multifaceted, incorporating a blend of accessible drugstore finds, prestigious brands,
potent active ingredients, and professional treatments. She's an educated consumer actively seeking out specific ingredients
known for their efficacy, including retinol, vitamin C, hyaluronic acid, peptides, ceramides, niacinamide, and broad-spectrum
SPF.
Her satisfaction is directly linked to tangible results - she expresses genuine enthusiasm for products that actually work. She
appreciates sophisticated formulations with lightweight, non-greasy textures that absorb well without feeling heavy or
clogging pores. A key driver of her positive sentiment is value - she loves finding affordable products that outperform their
more expensive counterparts.
What She Likes
Products that deliver visible results
Lightweight, non-greasy formulations that "melt in"
rather than sit on the skin
Science-based routines with proven ingredients
Trusted reviews from peers in her age group
Value-driven products that perform without excessive
cost
What She Dislikes
Feeling cheated by expensive products that don't deliver
Heavy, greasy textures that emphasize lines and pores
Young sales associates who lack understanding of
mature skin
Lack of regulation in the beauty industry
Unrealistic marketing claims and complex routines
Generic solutions that don't address her specific
concerns
"I used to love Bare Minerals Powder Foundation, but found as I got older, it kind of sat on the skin rather than blending in
and really emphasized all my pores. The Laura Geller really does melt in well, feels super lightweight, and is so quick to put
on."
"I have zero clue what to trust or which reviewers online to believe."
"It doesn't do anything. I paid two hundred something for a 30 milliliter of product and saw no results."

-- 6 of 8 --

The Efficacy Paradox: Skepticism vs. Hope
Overwhelmingly, this market is characterized by profound skepticism. A strong undercurrent of belief persists that most
topical skincare products will not work in any meaningful way. Many women in this demographic have concluded that the true
determinants of how one ages are non-negotiable factors like genetics, consistent sun protection, and a healthy lifestyle. They
believe that once a wrinkle is formed, no cream can magically erase it.
The reason for this disbelief is twofold. First, they believe the beauty industry is fundamentally built on selling "hope in a jar," a
metaphor that perfectly captures their view of exaggerated marketing claims and unfulfilled promises. Secondly, they feel the
industry has failed to formulate products that address the specific biological realities of their skin as estrogen declines.
99%
Skeptical of Claims
Nearly all women in this demographic
express doubt about marketing claims
made by skincare companies, based on
years of disappointment.
$1000s
Wasted Investment
The cumulative financial toll of decades
spent on products that yielded minimal
results creates a deep-seated wariness.
76%
Still Searching
Despite skepticism, the majority
continue to try new products, revealing
the paradoxical nature of their
relationship with skincare.
The Psychological State: Trapped Between
Skepticism and Hope
This consumer is trapped between her deep experience-based skepticism that products work and her persistent hopeful
search for a solution. She declares she won't be fooled by useless products, yet her purchasing habits show she is willing to try
a wide array of brands. She asserts that no cream can remove wrinkles, yet she actively seeks recommendations for products
that "actually work."
This paradox is not a sign of irrationality but a deeply human hope against experience. She is not looking for another fantasy -
she is searching for the one credible exception to the rule. The most compelling message will not be "this is magic" but rather
"we know you're skeptical, you have every right to be, here's the transparent, science-backed exception you've been searching
for."

-- 7 of 8 --

Historical Context and Forgotten Solutions
A powerful narrative that resonates with this demographic is the idea that their skin problems are not an inevitable part of
aging but distinctly modern afflictions. This belief is rooted in personal observations - they look at older generations, their
mothers and grandmothers, and see women who aged gracefully without access to the billion-dollar anti-aging industry.
"My mom is 65 and has gorgeous skin. She always says her secret is sunscreen and hats. My grandma's 85, looks quite a bit
younger, and did all the things in her younger years that you're not supposed to do - drank, smoked, tanned. The women on
that side of the family just had really good skin."
This suspicion is further solidified by reports of pre-industrialized or isolated cultures that appear to have far better skin,
reinforcing the idea that our contemporary lifestyle is the primary culprit. Studies and anecdotal reports from populations like
the Kitavan Islanders of Papua New Guinea and the Ache tribe of the Amazon reveal a near-total absence of acne and other
inflammatory skin conditions.
1	Ancient Beauty Wisdom
In ancient Greece, beauty was synonymous with
nature. Olive oil, honey, and herbs were
fundamental staples used not only in their diet but
also topically for cleansing, moisturizing, and
imparting a healthy glow.
2 Victorian Home Apothecary
Victorian-era beauty manuals provided recipes for
homemade options using ingredients like fresh
cucumber juice, almond oil, rose water, and witch
hazel. Cold creams were made from simple
ingredients like lard, wax, and scented oils without
synthetic preservatives.
3	Mid-Century Beauty Rituals
The beauty routines of the 1940s and 50s were not
about a single miracle product but a meticulous,
multi-step ritual emphasizing preservation and
gentle nourishment, contrasting with the modern
promise of instant results.
4 Forgotten Ingredients
Specific ingredients like bearberry extract (a
natural source of arbutin that lightens skin and
reduces hyperpigmentation) have been forgotten
by the mainstream beauty industry despite their
effectiveness and gentler nature compared to
synthetic alternatives.
These historical approaches to skincare offer compelling alternatives to the modern, often-disappointing skincare landscape.
The emphasis on natural ingredients, patient rituals, and holistic approaches resonates deeply with this demographic's
skepticism toward quick-fix solutions and preference for authenticity and transparency.

-- 8 of 8 --
